stem: A 52-year-old woman with BMI 34 and no alcohol history develops acute pancreatitis. Her ALT is 210 U/L. What is the most likely etiology, and what finding supports this?

choices.text: Gallstone pancreatitis — ALT >150 U/L within 48 hours has >85% positive predictive value for biliary etiology
Hypertriglyceridemia — elevated ALT indicates fatty liver with secondary pancreatic inflammation
Autoimmune pancreatitis — obesity and elevated liver enzymes suggest IgG4-related disease
Drug-induced pancreatitis — elevated ALT indicates hepatotoxic medication as the underlying cause

explanation: An ALT greater than 150 U/L within 48 hours of presentation has a positive predictive value exceeding 85% for gallstone pancreatitis. This elevated transaminase reflects hepatocyte injury from transient biliary obstruction by a migrating gallstone. In a patient with no alcohol history and risk factors for cholelithiasis (female, obesity, elevated BMI), gallstone etiology should be strongly suspected. Right upper quadrant ultrasound is the initial imaging study to identify gallstones, dilated common bile duct, or biliary sludge. MRCP or endoscopic ultrasound can further evaluate if suspicion remains high despite negative ultrasound.

stem: When ordering pancreatic enzymes for suspected acute pancreatitis, why is serum lipase preferred over amylase as the initial diagnostic marker?

choices.text: Lipase has greater sensitivity and specificity for pancreatic injury and remains elevated longer than amylase
Lipase is exclusively produced by the pancreas whereas amylase is exclusively produced by the salivary glands
Lipase levels directly correlate with disease severity and can guide the need for ICU admission
Amylase is no longer available as a standard laboratory assay at most institutions

explanation: Serum lipase is preferred over amylase for diagnosing acute pancreatitis because it has greater sensitivity and specificity. Lipase remains elevated longer than amylase (up to 14 days vs 3-5 days for amylase), making it more useful in delayed presentations. Amylase can be elevated in many non-pancreatic conditions (salivary gland disease, bowel obstruction, renal failure, macroamylasemia), reducing its specificity. Neither enzyme correlates with disease severity — a patient with mild lipase elevation can have severe necrotizing pancreatitis, and very high levels do not predict worse outcomes. Diagnosis requires lipase or amylase at least 3 times the upper limit of normal per the Revised Atlanta Classification.

stem: Early enteral nutrition in pancreatitis is preferred over TPN because:

choices.feedback: Nutrition doesn't change the disease course directly but reduces complications.

Correct! Enteral nutrition maintains intestinal mucosal barrier → prevents bacterial translocation → reduces infected necrosis and sepsis. Start oral feeding within 24h as tolerated. If unable to eat: nasojejunal tube feeding. TPN only as last resort (increases infection risk, cholestasis).
While true, the clinical rationale is more important.
Pain is not the distinguishing factor.

explanation: Early enteral nutrition (within 24 hours if tolerated) is preferred over TPN in acute pancreatitis because it maintains gut mucosal barrier integrity, preventing bacterial translocation from the gut lumen into the bloodstream. Gut barrier breakdown is a major contributor to infected pancreatic necrosis and systemic inflammatory response in severe pancreatitis. Enteral feeding also stimulates gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT), preserving immune function. Multiple randomized trials show enteral nutrition reduces infectious complications, organ failure, surgical interventions, and mortality compared to TPN. Oral feeding can be attempted first; nasojejunal tube feeding is used if oral intake is not tolerated.

stem: When are antibiotics indicated in acute pancreatitis?

choices.feedback: Leukocytosis is common in pancreatitis from inflammation alone and doesn't mandate antibiotics.

choices.text: Confirmed infected necrosis or concurrent cholangitis
choices.feedback: Correct! Antibiotics only for: infected necrosis (fever + gas on CT at 2-4 weeks → FNA confirms → carbapenems), or cholangitis (gallstone pancreatitis + Charcot triad → ERCP + antibiotics). Prophylactic antibiotics in sterile necrosis increase mortality.
Prophylactic antibiotics are NOT recommended — no benefit and increase resistant organisms/fungal infection.
Lipase level doesn't determine antibiotic need.

explanation: Antibiotics in acute pancreatitis are indicated only for confirmed infected pancreatic necrosis or concurrent cholangitis (ascending biliary infection). Prophylactic antibiotics are NOT recommended — multiple randomized controlled trials and meta-analyses show no mortality benefit and increased risk of fungal superinfection. Infected necrosis is suspected when clinical deterioration occurs (persistent fever, rising inflammatory markers) after the first 7-10 days. CT finding of gas within necrotic collections is highly suggestive. CT-guided fine-needle aspiration can confirm infection when clinical suspicion is equivocal. ERCP with sphincterotomy is indicated when cholangitis accompanies gallstone pancreatitis.

stem: A resident calculates a Ranson score for a patient admitted with acute alcoholic pancreatitis. Which limitation of this scoring system is most clinically significant?

choices.text: It requires 48 hours of data collection before the full score can be calculated, limiting early triage
It can only be applied to gallstone pancreatitis and is invalid for alcoholic pancreatitis
It does not incorporate any laboratory values and relies solely on clinical examination
It has been fully replaced by CT Severity Index in all current guidelines

explanation: The Ranson criteria is a widely known severity scoring system for acute pancreatitis, but its major limitation is that it requires a full 48 hours of data collection before the complete score can be calculated — 5 criteria at admission and 6 criteria at 48 hours. This delay limits its usefulness for early clinical decision-making. The BISAP score (BUN >25, impaired mental status, SIRS, age >60, pleural effusion) can be calculated at admission and is similarly predictive. The APACHE II score can also be applied early. The Revised Atlanta Classification focuses on organ failure duration (transient vs persistent >48 hours) as the primary determinant of severity.

stem: A patient with severe pancreatitis develops persistent fever on day 10. CT shows pancreatic necrosis with gas bubbles. What does this finding indicate and what is the next step?

choices.text: Infected pancreatic necrosis — requires CT-guided aspiration for culture and targeted antibiotics, with possible necrosectomy
Sterile pancreatic necrosis — gas is from tissue breakdown and requires only continued supportive care
Pancreatic pseudocyst formation — requires urgent surgical drainage regardless of symptoms
Pancreatic abscess — requires immediate open surgical debridement as first-line treatment

explanation: Persistent fever on day 10 of severe pancreatitis with CT showing gas within a necrotic collection is highly suggestive of infected pancreatic necrosis. Gas in the necrotic collection results from gas-producing bacteria (often enteric organisms from gut translocation). This is a critical distinction from sterile necrosis, which is managed conservatively. Infected necrosis requires intervention: the preferred modern approach is a 'step-up' strategy starting with percutaneous or endoscopic drainage, escalating to minimally invasive necrosectomy only if drainage fails. CT-guided fine needle aspiration with Gram stain and culture can confirm infection when clinical presentation is ambiguous. Direct open surgical debridement is now reserved as a last resort.

stem: A 38-year-old man is admitted with acute pancreatitis (lipase 950, HR 110, BP 100/65). What is the cornerstone of initial management?

choices.text: Aggressive IV crystalloid resuscitation with goal-directed fluid therapy targeting hemodynamic improvement
Emergent CT abdomen with contrast to grade severity before initiating any treatment
Prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics to prevent infected pancreatic necrosis
Urgent ERCP within 6 hours regardless of suspected etiology

explanation: Aggressive IV crystalloid resuscitation is the cornerstone of initial management in acute pancreatitis. Early goal-directed fluid therapy corrects hypovolemia (from third-spacing, vomiting, and increased vascular permeability), maintains pancreatic microcirculation, and prevents organ failure. Lactated Ringer's solution is preferred over normal saline as it may have anti-inflammatory properties and reduces the risk of hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis. Fluid resuscitation should be initiated immediately and titrated to clinical endpoints (urine output, heart rate, mean arterial pressure). CT imaging is NOT needed emergently — it is most useful 72-96 hours after onset for assessing necrosis. Prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated. ERCP is reserved for concurrent cholangitis.

stem: What distinguishes biliary colic from acute cholecystitis?

choices.feedback: Stone type doesn't differentiate the two clinically.
Both occur in similar age groups.
Both cause RUQ pain. Location alone doesn't differentiate.

Correct! Biliary colic: episodic RUQ pain <6h after fatty meal, self-resolves, no fever/Murphy sign. Cholecystitis: constant pain >6h, fever, positive Murphy sign, leukocytosis. Cholecystitis = inflammation, biliary colic = transient obstruction without inflammation.

explanation: Biliary colic is self-limited RUQ pain lasting <6 hours (typically 30 minutes to a few hours) caused by transient cystic duct obstruction by a gallstone. It resolves when the stone disimpacts. Acute cholecystitis develops when the obstruction persists (>6 hours), leading to gallbladder wall inflammation, edema, and potential ischemia/necrosis. Distinguishing features of cholecystitis vs colic: duration >6 hours, fever, positive Murphy sign on exam, leukocytosis, and persistent/worsening pain. Biliary colic patients are afebrile with normal labs and pain that resolves. Both may show gallstones on ultrasound, but wall thickening and pericholecystic fluid favor cholecystitis.

stem: #1 cause of small bowel obstruction?

choices.feedback: Crohn strictures can cause SBO but account for only ~5%.

Correct! Post-surgical adhesions cause ~60% of SBO. Even a single prior abdominal surgery creates adhesion risk. In patients without prior surgery, hernias are the #1 cause. Always ask about surgical history in a patient with SBO symptoms.
Cancer is the #1 cause of LARGE bowel obstruction.
Intussusception is more common in children (lead point from Meckel diverticulum or lymphoma in adults).

explanation: Adhesions from prior abdominal surgery are the most common cause of small bowel obstruction (SBO), accounting for approximately 60-70% of cases. Adhesive bands form during the healing process after any peritoneal disruption. Other causes include hernias (~15%, second most common — and the most common cause of SBO in patients without prior surgery), malignancy (~10%), Crohn's disease, and intussusception. Management of adhesive SBO depends on whether there are signs of strangulation: uncomplicated partial SBO can be managed with NPO, NGT decompression, IV fluids, and observation. Complete obstruction or signs of strangulation require operative intervention.

stem: Sigmoid volvulus on X-ray shows:

choices.feedback: Apple-core = annular colorectal carcinoma on barium enema.
Free air suggests perforation, a complication rather than volvulus itself.
This suggests SBO, not sigmoid volvulus.

Correct! Sigmoid volvulus: massively dilated sigmoid loop rising from the pelvis, creating a 'coffee bean' or inverted U shape. The loop often extends up toward the diaphragm. Inner wall creates the 'crease' of the coffee bean. First-line treatment in stable patient: sigmoidoscopic decompression.

explanation: Sigmoid volvulus on abdominal X-ray produces the classic 'coffee bean' or 'omega' sign — a massively dilated loop of sigmoid colon that extends from the pelvis toward the right upper quadrant, with the apex pointing toward the right diaphragm. The walls of the distended loop converge at the point of torsion in the left lower quadrant. Sigmoid volvulus accounts for ~60-75% of all colonic volvulus cases and is more common in elderly, institutionalized, or neuropsychiatric patients with chronic constipation and a redundant sigmoid. Initial treatment for uncomplicated sigmoid volvulus is endoscopic detorsion (flexible sigmoidoscopy), followed by semi-elective sigmoid resection to prevent recurrence (~60% recurrence without surgery).

stem: Cecal volvulus treatment differs from sigmoid because:

choices.feedback: Correct! Unlike sigmoid volvulus (which can be decompressed via rigid sigmoidoscopy), cecal volvulus is treated surgically (right hemicolectomy or cecopexy). Endoscopic decompression of cecal volvulus has high failure rate and risk of perforation. Delay in surgery increases mortality.
Cecal volvulus occurs in adults with a mobile cecum.
Cecal volvulus does NOT resolve spontaneously and requires intervention.
Antibiotics don't treat mechanical obstruction.

explanation: Cecal volvulus differs from sigmoid volvulus in that it generally cannot be decompressed endoscopically — the colonoscope cannot reach or effectively reduce the cecal torsion. Cecal volvulus typically requires surgical management (right hemicolectomy or cecopexy). In contrast, sigmoid volvulus can usually be detorsed with flexible sigmoidoscopy as an initial temporizing measure. Cecal volvulus is less common than sigmoid volvulus (~25-40% of colonic volvulus) and occurs in younger patients. X-ray shows a 'kidney-bean' shaped distended cecum displaced to the left upper quadrant (the twist moves the cecum from its normal RLQ position).

stem: Ogilvie syndrome treatment?

choices.feedback: Correct! Neostigmine inhibits acetylcholinesterase → increases parasympathetic tone → stimulates colonic motility. ~90% response rate. Monitor with telemetry (risk of bradycardia — have atropine ready). If neostigmine fails: colonoscopic decompression. Surgery for perforation or failed conservative measures.
Antibiotics don't treat pseudo-obstruction.
Ogilvie's is a motility disorder, not a simple constipation problem.
Surgery is reserved for failure of medical therapy or perforation.

explanation: Ogilvie syndrome (acute colonic pseudo-obstruction) is treated with IV neostigmine. Ogilvie syndrome presents as massive colonic dilation without mechanical obstruction, typically in hospitalized patients with severe illness, postoperative state, or electrolyte abnormalities. Neostigmine (an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) restores colonic motility by enhancing parasympathetic activity. It has ~80-90% initial success rate. Administration requires cardiac monitoring due to risk of bradycardia (atropine should be available). Conservative management (NPO, rectal tube, correction of electrolytes, cessation of offending medications like opioids and anticholinergics) is tried first. Cecal diameter >12 cm or failure of conservative/pharmacologic therapy warrants colonoscopic decompression or surgery.

stem: Strangulated bowel obstruction — key clinical finding?

choices.feedback: Pain relief in obstruction can paradoxically indicate perforation (pressure release), not improvement.

choices.text: Continuous (non-colicky) pain with peritoneal signs, fever,
choices.feedback: Correct! Strangulation = compromised mesenteric blood supply → ischemia → necrosis → perforation. Changes from colicky to continuous pain. Peritoneal signs (guarding, rebound), fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis, lactic acidosis. CT: bowel wall thickening, mesenteric haziness, pneumatosis. EMERGENT surgery required.
Colicky pain suggests simple obstruction. Strangulation causes continuous pain.
Painless vomiting suggests gastroparesis, not strangulated obstruction.

explanation: Strangulated bowel obstruction — where the blood supply is compromised — is indicated by continuous (non-colicky) pain with peritoneal signs (guarding, rigidity, rebound tenderness). In simple mechanical obstruction, pain is characteristically colicky/intermittent (corresponding to peristaltic waves against the obstruction). The transition to continuous pain suggests ischemia or perforation. Other signs of strangulation include fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis with left shift, metabolic acidosis (rising lactate), and peritoneal signs. Strangulated obstruction requires emergent surgery — delay leads to bowel necrosis, perforation, and sepsis. CT findings include mesenteric haziness, bowel wall thickening, reduced enhancement, and pneumatosis.

The transition to continuous pain suggests ischemia or perforation. stem: Water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) in SBO is both:

choices.text: Diagnostic for cancer and therapeutic for adhesions
choices.feedback: Gastrografin doesn't diagnose cancer specifically.

choices.text: Therapeutic (osmotic effect aids resolution) AND prognostic (contrast in colon at 24h predicts resolution)
choices.feedback: Correct! Gastrografin has osmotic properties that draw fluid into the bowel lumen → reduces edema → may help resolve partial adhesive SBO. If contrast reaches colon within 24h = likely to resolve without surgery. If not = higher likelihood of needing operative intervention.
Water-soluble contrast is safe. BARIUM is relatively contraindicated (risk of barium peritonitis if perforation).

choices.text: Only diagnostic with no therapeutic benefit
choices.feedback: Gastrografin has both diagnostic and therapeutic properties.

explanation: Water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) administered orally in adhesive SBO serves dual roles: therapeutic — its hyperosmolar nature draws fluid into the bowel lumen, which promotes motility and may hasten resolution of partial obstruction (reduces the need for surgery), and prognostic — if contrast reaches the colon within 24 hours on follow-up X-ray, the obstruction is likely to resolve non-operatively. Failure of contrast to reach the colon predicts the need for surgical intervention. Multiple studies show that Gastrografin reduces hospital length of stay and may reduce the need for surgery in adhesive SBO. It should not be used if strangulation is suspected.

stem: Cecal diameter >9 cm on imaging indicates:

choices.feedback: Correct! Rule of 3-6-9: small bowel >3 cm, colon >6 cm, cecum >9 cm = abnormally dilated. Cecum >9 cm (some say >12 cm) = high risk of perforation due to Law of Laplace (wall tension increases with diameter). Requires urgent decompression.
Cecal dilation is an acute finding, not a screening indication.
While volvulus causes dilation, diameter alone doesn't confirm the diagnosis.
Cecal diameter >9 cm is never normal.

explanation: Cecal diameter exceeding 9 cm on imaging indicates significant risk of perforation, particularly in the setting of colonic obstruction or pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie syndrome). The cecum is the most common site of colonic perforation in distal obstruction because of Laplace's law: wall tension is proportional to radius, so the cecum (the widest part of the colon) experiences the greatest wall tension. Some sources use 12 cm as the critical threshold for imminent perforation risk, while others use 9-10 cm. Cecal diameter should be monitored serially with abdominal X-rays. Exceeding the threshold warrants urgent decompression (endoscopic or surgical).

stem: Closed-loop obstruction requires emergent surgery because:

choices.text: It only occurs in the small bowel
choices.feedback: Closed-loop can occur in both small and large bowel.
Antibiotic penetration is not the issue.
Closed-loop can occur from volvulus, hernia, or adhesive bands.

choices.text: Bowel is obstructed at two points → rapid pressure rise → ischemia/perforation
choices.feedback: Correct! Two points of obstruction trap a bowel segment → intraluminal pressure rises rapidly (no decompression possible) → venous then arterial compromise → ischemia → gangrene → perforation. CT shows C-shaped or U-shaped loop with mesenteric whirl sign. This is a surgical emergency.

explanation: Closed-loop obstruction requires emergent surgery because bowel is obstructed at TWO points (creating a sealed segment), causing rapid pressure buildup, vascular compromise, and ischemia. Without an outlet for decompression, intraluminal pressure rises quickly, compressing mesenteric veins first (causing venous congestion and edema) then arteries (causing ischemic necrosis and perforation). This progresses much faster than simple mechanical obstruction. Common causes include adhesive bands creating a closed loop, incarcerated hernias, and sigmoid volvulus. CT shows the 'whirl sign' (twisted mesentery) and a U-shaped or C-shaped dilated loop. Closed-loop obstruction cannot be managed conservatively.

stem: First-line imaging for suspected acute cholecystitis?

choices.feedback: Only 10-15% of gallstones are radiopaque. X-ray is insensitive.
MRCP evaluates bile ducts (choledocholithiasis), not primary cholecystitis.
CT is less sensitive than US for gallstones and cholecystitis.

Correct! RUQ US is first-line: fast, no radiation, detects gallstones (95% sensitivity), wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy sign. HIDA scan is gold standard if US is equivocal (non-visualization of GB = cystic duct obstruction).

explanation: Right upper quadrant (RUQ) ultrasound is the first-line imaging study for suspected acute cholecystitis. Findings include gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening (>3 mm), pericholecystic fluid, gallbladder distension, and a sonographic Murphy sign (focal tenderness when the ultrasound probe is pressed directly over the gallbladder). Ultrasound has sensitivity of ~80-90% and specificity of ~80% for acute cholecystitis. If ultrasound is equivocal, HIDA scan (cholescintigraphy) is the most specific test — non-visualization of the gallbladder after 4 hours (with CCK or morphine augmentation) indicates cystic duct obstruction, with sensitivity and specificity both >95%.

Findings include gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening (>3 mm), pericholecystic fluid, gallbladder distension, and a sonographic Murphy sign (focal tenderness when the ultrasound probe is pressed directly over the gallbladder). Ultrasound has sensitivity of ~80-90% and specificity of ~80% for acute cholecystitis. If ultrasound is equivocal, HIDA scan (cholescintigraphy) is the most specific test — non-visualization of the gallbladder after 4 hours (with CCK or morphine augmentation) indicates cystic duct obstruction, with sensitivity and specificity both >95%.

stem: Acalculous cholecystitis — classic setting?

choices.feedback: That's classic for symptomatic cholelithiasis, not acalculous.
Post-ERCP pancreatitis is more common than post-ERCP acalculous cholecystitis.
Pregnancy increases cholesterol stone risk, not acalculous cholecystitis.

Correct! Acalculous cholecystitis: GB inflammation without stones in critically ill patients. Mechanism: biliary stasis + GB ischemia from hypoperfusion. Mortality 30-50%. Treatment: percutaneous cholecystostomy (temporizing — many are too sick for OR). HIDA scan best diagnostic test.

explanation: Acalculous cholecystitis occurs in critically ill ICU patients (5-10% of all cholecystitis cases) — typically those with prolonged hospitalization, sepsis, total parenteral nutrition, mechanical ventilation, or major burns/trauma. Without gallstones, the mechanism involves gallbladder stasis and ischemia from splanchnic hypoperfusion. Diagnosis is challenging because ICU patients cannot report symptoms reliably. Ultrasound findings include gallbladder distension, wall thickening, and pericholecystic fluid without stones. Acalculous cholecystitis carries higher mortality than calculous cholecystitis (~30-50% vs ~1-3%) due to the underlying critical illness and delayed diagnosis. Treatment is percutaneous cholecystostomy or cholecystectomy.

stem: Charcot triad for cholangitis:

choices.feedback: Correct! Charcot triad = cholangitis (infected bile duct, usually from CBD stone). RUQ pain + fever + jaundice. Present in ~70% of cases. Reynolds pentad adds AMS + hypotension (septic cholangitis → emergent ERCP for biliary drainage). Do not delay decompression.
This describes meningitis.
This describes pulmonary embolism (Virchow triad concepts).
These are nonspecific GI symptoms.

explanation: Charcot's triad for ascending cholangitis consists of: RUQ pain, fever, and jaundice. This represents infection of the biliary system, most commonly from choledocholithiasis (common bile duct stone) causing obstruction and subsequent bacterial overgrowth. Charcot's triad is present in only about 50-70% of cholangitis cases. The most common causative organisms are E. coli, Klebsiella, and Enterococcus. Treatment requires antibiotics plus biliary decompression (ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction is preferred). Labs show elevated bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, GGT, and often transaminases.

stem: Gallstone ileus — classic imaging triad?

choices.feedback: This describes biliary obstruction with decompensation, not gallstone ileus.
Coffee-bean = sigmoid volvulus.

choices.text: SBO + pneumobilia (air in biliary tree) + ectopic gallstone
choices.feedback: Correct! Rigler triad: SBO (dilated small bowel) + pneumobilia (air in biliary tree from cholecystoenteric fistula) + ectopic gallstone (usually impacted at ileocecal valve — narrowest part of small bowel). Gallstone erodes through GB wall into duodenum → travels distally → impacts. Rare but classic.
This suggests bowel ischemia with perforation.

explanation: Gallstone ileus is a mechanical small bowel obstruction caused by a gallstone that has eroded through the gallbladder wall into the adjacent duodenum (cholecystoduodenal fistula) and traveled distally to obstruct the bowel, most commonly at the ileocecal valve (the narrowest point). The classic Rigler's triad on imaging includes: small bowel obstruction, pneumobilia (air in the biliary tree, from the fistula), and an ectopic gallstone (calcified density in the bowel). This triad is pathognomonic but present in only ~40-50% of cases. Gallstone ileus is rare (~1-4% of SBO cases) but carries high mortality due to the elderly population it typically affects.

stem: Emphysematous cholecystitis — key association?

choices.feedback: Emphysematous cholecystitis is most common in diabetic males.
While immunosuppression increases risk, diabetes is the classic association.
PPIs are not associated with emphysematous cholecystitis.

Correct! Emphysematous cholecystitis: gas-producing organisms (Clostridium, E. coli) infect an ischemic GB wall. Risk: diabetes, male sex, elderly. Imaging: air in GB wall/lumen. 15-20% perforation rate. Treatment: EMERGENT cholecystectomy (high mortality without surgery).

explanation: Emphysematous cholecystitis — gas-forming infection of the gallbladder wall — is classically associated with diabetic men. It is caused by gas-producing organisms (Clostridium spp., E. coli, Klebsiella) and represents a surgical emergency with high perforation and mortality risk (~15-20%). CT is the best imaging modality, showing gas within the gallbladder wall or lumen. Unlike typical cholecystitis, emphysematous cholecystitis requires emergent cholecystectomy (not elective or interval surgery) plus broad-spectrum antibiotics. Acalculous disease is more common in emphysematous cholecystitis than in typical cholecystitis.

Acalculous disease is more common in emphysematous cholecystitis than in typical cholecystitis.

stem: Reynolds pentad adds which TWO features to Charcot triad?

choices.feedback: While present, these are not the defining additions to Reynolds pentad.

Correct! Reynolds pentad = Charcot triad (RUQ pain + fever + jaundice) + AMS + hypotension. Indicates septic cholangitis — biliary sepsis. This is an emergency requiring immediate ERCP for biliary decompression. Mortality is high without drainage.
GI symptoms don't define Reynolds pentad.
These suggest chronic liver disease, not acute cholangitis.

explanation: Reynolds' pentad adds altered mental status and hypotension (septic shock) to Charcot's triad (RUQ pain, fever, jaundice), indicating severe suppurative cholangitis with sepsis. Reynolds' pentad is present in only about 5-10% of cholangitis cases but signifies life-threatening biliary sepsis requiring emergent biliary decompression (ERCP). These patients are hemodynamically unstable and require aggressive resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and urgent source control. Delayed biliary drainage in Reynolds' pentad carries high mortality.

stem: Timing of cholecystectomy in acute cholecystitis?

choices.text: Only if antibiotics fail after 2 weeks
choices.feedback: Surgery is first-line, not a fallback after antibiotic failure.

Correct! Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy (within 72h) is preferred over delayed. Benefits: shorter total hospital stay, lower morbidity, lower conversion to open rate, cost-effective. Antibiotics + early surgery is standard of care.
Cholecystectomy is definitive. Antibiotics alone have high recurrence rates.

choices.text: Delay 6-8 weeks for inflammation to resolve
choices.feedback: Studies show early surgery is superior — delayed surgery has higher complications.

explanation: Early cholecystectomy (within 72 hours, ideally within 24 hours of diagnosis) is recommended for acute cholecystitis per current guidelines. Early surgery has equivalent safety to delayed surgery, shorter total hospital stay, fewer readmissions, and lower overall cost. Delaying surgery beyond 72 hours increases technical difficulty due to progressive inflammation and fibrosis. Laparoscopic approach is preferred. For patients who are poor surgical candidates (severe comorbidities), percutaneous cholecystostomy tube provides temporizing drainage. The previous practice of 'cooling off' with antibiotics and interval cholecystectomy at 6-8 weeks is no longer recommended as first-line.

stem: Classic CSF finding in GBS?

choices.feedback: Xanthochromia indicates subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Oligoclonal bands are characteristic of multiple sclerosis.

Correct! Elevated protein from nerve root inflammation + normal WBC count. This dissociation is characteristic of GBS. May be normal in the first week — repeat LP if initial is normal and clinical suspicion remains high.
That's bacterial meningitis. GBS has normal cell count.

explanation: Albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated CSF protein with normal white cell count) is the characteristic CSF finding in GBS. It reflects inflammatory demyelination of nerve roots within the subarachnoid space, increasing protein content without meningeal cellular inflammation. The finding may be absent early (<1 week) and is most reliable after the first week of illness. Normal CSF early in the course does not exclude GBS.

stem: Most common preceding infection in GBS?

choices.feedback: GAS → rheumatic fever/glomerulonephritis, not GBS.

Correct! C. jejuni gastroenteritis precedes ~30% of GBS cases. Molecular mimicry between bacterial lipooligosaccharides and gangliosides on peripheral nerves triggers autoimmune attack. Anti-GM1 antibodies associated with axonal variants and worse prognosis.
E. coli is associated with UTI/meningitis, not GBS.
Pneumococcus causes pneumonia/meningitis, not typically GBS.

explanation: Campylobacter jejuni is the most commonly identified preceding infection in GBS, occurring in approximately 25-40% of cases. Molecular mimicry between bacterial lipooligosaccharides and peripheral nerve gangliosides triggers the autoimmune response. GBS typically manifests 1-3 weeks after the inciting infection.

stem: When to intubate a GBS patient?

choices.feedback: Far too late — proactive monitoring and early intubation are essential.
Intubation is based on objective respiratory parameters, not patient preference.
Hypoxia is a LATE sign — intubate based on respiratory mechanics, not oxygenation.

Correct! The 20/30 rule: FVC <target rate or negative inspiratory force (NIF) weaker than -30 cmH2O. Monitor FVC serially q4-6h. Rapid decline (>30% over 24h) also warrants intubation. 30% of GBS patients will need mechanical ventilation.

explanation: Intubation in GBS is guided by the 20/30 rule: FVC <20 mL/kg or NIF weaker than -30 cmH2O. Serial monitoring every 4-6 hours is critical as respiratory failure can progress rapidly. Approximately 30% of GBS patients require mechanical ventilation.

stem: Treatment NOT effective in GBS?

choices.feedback: Supportive care including respiratory monitoring is essential.

Correct! Unlike most autoimmune conditions, corticosteroids are NOT effective in GBS and may worsen outcomes. This distinguishes GBS from CIDP (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), where steroids ARE effective. Treatment options: IVIG OR plasmapheresis (not both together).
IVIG is first-line treatment for GBS.
Plasmapheresis is equally effective as IVIG for GBS.

explanation: Corticosteroids have no proven benefit in GBS and may delay recovery. Effective treatments are IVIG and plasmapheresis, which are equally effective. This distinguishes GBS from CIDP, where steroids are first-line.

stem: GBS weakness pattern is:

choices.feedback: Unilateral weakness suggests stroke, not GBS.

choices.text: Descending from cranial nerves to legs
choices.feedback: Descending pattern is more typical of myasthenia gravis or botulism.

Correct! Classic ascending paralysis: starts in feet/legs → thighs → trunk → arms → cranial nerves → respiratory muscles. Bilateral and relatively symmetric. Areflexia is the hallmark physical exam finding. Peak severity reached within 2-4 weeks.
Fatigable weakness is characteristic of myasthenia gravis, not GBS.

explanation: GBS weakness follows an ascending pattern, starting distally in the lower extremities and progressing proximally to the upper extremities, trunk, and cranial nerves. This reflects the peripheral nerve demyelination pattern affecting the longest nerves first. The ascending pattern helps distinguish GBS from botulism (descending) and myasthenia gravis (fluctuating, fatigable, ocular-predominant).

stem: Why should IVIG and plasmapheresis NOT be combined?

choices.text: Plasmapheresis removes the infused IVIG from circulation
choices.feedback: Correct! PLEX removes plasma proteins including the therapeutic IVIG antibodies, negating its benefit. Choose one treatment. Both are equally effective as monotherapy. IVIG is often preferred due to ease of administration (no need for large-bore vascular access/apheresis machine).
The clinical rationale, not coverage, is why they shouldn't be combined.
Not the primary reason for avoiding combination.
There's no pharmacologic interaction per se.

explanation: Plasmapheresis physically removes circulating immunoglobulins, including any IVIG that has been administered. Combining the two treatments is counterproductive. Either treatment alone is effective; they should be used sequentially only if the first-line choice fails.

stem: Key feature distinguishing GBS from myasthenia gravis?

choices.feedback: Cholinesterase inhibitors (pyridostigmine) treat MG, not GBS.
Only myasthenic crisis requires ventilation; many MG patients are well-controlled.
GBS has areflexia/hyporeflexia; MG has normal reflexes.

Correct! GBS: ascending, areflexic, can have sensory symptoms, CSF protein elevated, preceding infection. MG: descending (starts bulbar — ptosis, diplopia, dysarthria → limbs), fatigable weakness (worsens with repetitive use), normal reflexes, no sensory involvement, anti-AChR/MuSK antibodies.

explanation: Areflexia is the key feature distinguishing GBS from myasthenia gravis (MG). GBS affects peripheral nerve myelin/axons causing absent reflexes, while MG is a neuromuscular junction disorder with preserved reflexes. MG also features fatigable weakness (worsens with repetition, improves with rest), ptosis, and diplopia. GBS weakness is constant and does not fluctuate with activity. Nerve conduction studies show demyelination in GBS vs decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation in MG.

stem: Prognosis of GBS — what percentage walk independently at 1 year?

choices.feedback: Correct! ~80% of GBS patients walk independently at 1 year. Most recovery occurs in the first 6-12 months. Poor prognostic factors: older age, rapid progression, need for ventilation, preceding C. jejuni infection (axonal variant), low compound muscle action potential amplitudes on EMG.
Recovery rate is higher than 50%.
GBS has a much better prognosis than this.
While most recover well, 5-10% have significant residual disability and 3-5% die.

explanation: Approximately 80% of GBS patients walk independently at 1 year. GBS has generally favorable prognosis: recovery begins 2-4 weeks after symptom nadir. However, ~5% mortality persists (from respiratory failure, autonomic dysfunction, pulmonary embolism), ~10-20% have significant residual disability, and up to 5% relapse. Poor prognostic factors include older age, rapid onset, preceding Campylobacter infection, need for mechanical ventilation, and axonal variant (AMAN).

stem: Most common type of headache?

choices.feedback: Correct! Tension-type headache is the most common primary headache disorder. Bilateral, band-like, pressing/tightening quality, mild-moderate. No nausea/vomiting, no aura. Distinguished from migraine by the absence of unilateral pulsating quality, nausea, and photo/phonophobia.
Cluster headaches are relatively rare but extremely severe.
Migraines are common but tension-type is the most prevalent.
Medication overuse headache is common but secondary, not primary.

explanation: Tension-type headache (TTH) is the most common primary headache, affecting approximately 40% of the population. It presents as bilateral, pressing/tightening (non-pulsating), mild-to-moderate pain without nausea/vomiting or significant photo/phonophobia. It is not aggravated by routine physical activity (unlike migraine). TTH is more common than migraine, cluster headache, or other primary headache types.

stem: New headache in a patient >50 with jaw claudication and elevated ESR. Immediate step?

choices.text: Temporal artery biopsy first, then treat based on results
choices.feedback: Delaying treatment risks permanent vision loss.
GCA requires immediate steroids, not NSAIDs.

Correct! Giant cell arteritis (temporal arteritis): risk of irreversible vision loss from ophthalmic artery involvement. Start prednisoneMEDIATELY. Do NOT wait for temporal artery biopsy (gold standard — remains positive for 1-2 weeks after starting steroids). Biopsy may show skip lesions (sample ≥2 cm).
MRI doesn't diagnose GCA. The immediate concern is preventing vision loss.

explanation: New headache in a patient >50 with jaw claudication and elevated ESR strongly suggests giant cell arteritis (GCA/temporal arteritis), requiring immediate high-dose corticosteroids (prednisone 60-80 mg/day or IV methylprednisolone if vision threatened) to prevent irreversible vision loss from anterior ischemic optic neuropathy. Treatment should NOT wait for temporal artery biopsy (which remains positive for 1-2 weeks after starting steroids). ESR >50 mm/hr and CRP elevation support diagnosis. Visual symptoms are the most feared complication.

stem: Cushing triad indicates:

choices.feedback: Cushing disease/syndrome is adrenal. Cushing TRIAD is a separate neurologic entity.

Correct! Cushing triad = last-ditch effort by brainstem to maintain cerebral perfusion against rising ICP: hypertension (to force blood past elevated ICP), reflexive bradycardia (baroreceptor response), irregular respirations (brainstem compression). This is a pre-terminal sign — emergent intervention required.
Not related to Cushing triad.
Not related to Cushing triad.

explanation: Cushing triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations) indicates critically elevated intracranial pressure with imminent brainstem herniation. It represents a late and ominous sign — the brainstem response to ischemia from herniation. Hypertension is a reflex attempt to maintain cerebral perfusion pressure, bradycardia results from vagal nerve compression, and irregular respirations reflect medullary dysfunction. This requires immediate intervention (osmotic therapy, hyperventilation, neurosurgical decompression).

stem: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri) typical patient?

choices.feedback: IIH predominantly affects young obese women.

choices.text: Thin elderly woman with temporal headache
choices.feedback: That profile suggests temporal arteritis/GCA.

choices.text: Obese young woman with headache and papilledema
choices.feedback: Correct! Classic demographics: obese women of childbearing age. Headache, papilledema (bilateral), pulsatile tinnitus, transient visual obscurations. Normal MRI. LP: opening pressure >25 cm H₂O with normal CSF. Associated drugs: tetracyclines, vitamin A/retinoids, growth hormone. Treatment: weight loss, acetazolamide.
Exercise headache is a different entity.

explanation: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH/pseudotumor cerebri) typically affects obese women of childbearing age (20-40). Classic presentation: headache (often positional), papilledema on fundoscopy, visual changes (transient visual obscurations, enlarged blind spots), and pulsatile tinnitus. Diagnosis requires elevated LP opening pressure (>25 cmH2O) with normal CSF composition and normal neuroimaging. Treatment: weight loss, acetazolamide (reduces CSF production), serial LPs for acute relief, and optic nerve sheath fenestration or CSF shunting for refractory cases.

stem: CN VI palsy with increased ICP is called a 'false localizing sign' because:

choices.text: CN VI is never affected by ICP
choices.feedback: CN VI is actually commonly affected, which is why it's a false localizer.

choices.text: CN VI is too small to be affected by mass lesions
choices.feedback: Size is not the issue; it's the vulnerability from the long course.

choices.text: The long intracranial course makes CN VI vulnerable to diffuse ICP elevation, not a focal lesion
choices.feedback: Correct! CN VI (abducens) has the longest intracranial course of any cranial nerve. Diffusely elevated ICP stretches it over the petrous ridge → lateral rectus palsy → inability to abduct the eye. This occurs with generalized ICP elevation, not necessarily a lesion at that specific location — hence 'false localizing.'

choices.text: The palsy is always bilateral, indicating diffuse disease
choices.feedback: Can be unilateral or bilateral. The key is that it doesn't indicate the lesion location.

explanation: CN VI (abducens) palsy is a classic false localizing sign in increased ICP. The abducens nerve has the longest intracranial course of any cranial nerve, running along the clivus and through Dorello canal, making it vulnerable to stretching and compression from diffuse ICP elevation anywhere in the cranium — not just from a focal lesion near the sixth nerve nucleus. This means CN VI palsy does not reliably localize the lesion causing the increased ICP.

stem: Triptans are contraindicated in:

choices.feedback: Age alone is not a contraindication — cardiovascular risk assessment determines eligibility.

choices.text: Patients who have already taken NSAIDs
choices.feedback: Triptans can be safely combined with or used after NSAIDs.

choices.text: Patients with coronary artery disease or uncontrolled HTN
choices.feedback: Correct! Triptans are 5-HT1B/1D agonists → vasoconstriction. Contraindicated in: CAD, prior MI/stroke, uncontrolled HTN, basilar/hemiplegic migraine, peripheral vascular disease. Also avoid within 24h of ergotamine. Risk of serotonin syndrome if combined with SSRIs (low but real).
Triptans are simply ineffective for tension headache, not necessarily contraindicated.

explanation: Triptans (5-HT1B/1D receptor agonists) cause vasoconstriction and are contraindicated in coronary artery disease, cerebrovascular disease, uncontrolled hypertension, peripheral vascular disease, and hemiplegic/basilar migraine (risk of stroke). They should not be used within 24 hours of ergotamine derivatives. Triptans are effective for acute migraine and cluster headache but their vasoconstrictive properties make them dangerous in patients with vascular disease.

stem: Hyperventilation reduces ICP by:

choices.text: Increasing oxygen delivery to the brain
choices.feedback: The mechanism is CO2-mediated vasoconstriction, not oxygen delivery.
While hyperventilation affects metabolism, the ICP mechanism is vasoconstriction.
Hyperventilation does not directly affect CSF dynamics.

Correct! Reducing PCO2 from ~40 to 30-35 mmHg causes cerebral arteriolar vasoconstriction → decreased cerebral blood volume → rapid ICP reduction. TEMPORARY effect only (hours) — brain compensates. Used as a bridge to definitive treatment (mannitol, surgery). Target PCO2 30-35 mmHg. Excessive hyperventilation (<25) risks cerebral ischemia.

explanation: Hyperventilation reduces ICP by lowering arterial PCO2, which causes cerebral vasoconstriction, reducing cerebral blood volume and thus intracranial pressure. Target PCO2 is 30-35 mmHg. This is a temporizing measure only (effective for minutes to hours) as the brain adapts by adjusting CSF bicarbonate. Excessive or prolonged hyperventilation (PCO2 <25 mmHg) can cause dangerous cerebral ischemia. It is used as a bridge to definitive treatment (surgery, osmotic therapy).

stem: FIRST intervention in hyperkalemia with EKG changes?

choices.feedback: Correct! Calcium gluconate stabilizes the cardiac membrane within 1-3 minutes by raising the threshold potential (antagonizes K+ effect on cardiac conduction). Does NOT lower serum K+. Repeat in 5 min if EKG unchanged. Then proceed to K+-lowering measures.
Kayexalate removes K+ but takes hours — too slow for acute emergency.
Diuretics help excrete K+ but don't protect the heart immediately.
Insulin shifts K+ into cells but doesn't immediately stabilize the heart.

explanation: The FIRST intervention in hyperkalemia with EKG changes is IV calcium gluconate. Calcium does NOT lower potassium — it stabilizes the cardiac membrane by restoring the transmembrane voltage gradient, protecting against lethal arrhythmias. Onset is 1-3 minutes, duration 30-60 minutes. This buys time for potassium-lowering therapies to take effect. StatPearls states: 'Calcium gluconate is generally preferred as the initial agent in patients with evidence of cardiac toxicity.' The treatment sequence is: (1) IV calcium gluconate — membrane stabilization (immediate); (2) IV insulin + glucose — shifts K+ intracellularly (onset 20-30 min); (3) Albuterol nebulization — additional intracellular shift; (4) Kayexalate/patiromer/SZC — potassium elimination (slow onset); (5) Hemodialysis — definitive removal if refractory. Kayexalate, furosemide, and insulin all lower K+ but do NOT protect the heart immediately.

stem: Earliest EKG change in hyperkalemia?

choices.feedback: ST elevation is associated with ischemia/pericarditis, not hyperkalemia.
Wide QRS occurs later (K+ 7.0-8.0).

Correct! Peaked, tent-shaped T waves are the earliest and most sensitive EKG finding (K+ ~5.5-6.5). Progression: peaked T → PR prolongation → P wave loss → QRS widening → sine wave → VF/asystole. Any EKG change = treat immediately.
Prolonged QT is more associated with HYPOkalemia (U waves).

explanation: The earliest EKG change in hyperkalemia is peaked, narrow T waves (especially in precordial leads V2-V4). This occurs because elevated extracellular K+ accelerates phase 3 repolarization of the action potential. The classic progression of hyperkalemic EKG changes with rising K+ is: (1) Peaked T waves (5.5-6.5 mEq/L); (2) Prolonged PR interval (6.5-7.0); (3) Loss of P waves (7.0-7.5); (4) Widened QRS (7.5-8.0); (5) Sine wave pattern (>8.0); (6) Asystole/VF. ST elevation is associated with MI or pericarditis, not hyperkalemia. Widened QRS is a LATE finding of hyperkalemia, not early. Prolonged QT is associated with hypokalemia and hypocalcemia, not hyperkalemia (hyperkalemia actually shortens the QT interval initially).
references: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

ST elevation is associated with MI or pericarditis, not hyperkalemia. Widened QRS is a LATE finding of hyperkalemia, not early. Prolonged QT is associated with hypokalemia and hypocalcemia, not hyperkalemia (hyperkalemia actually shortens the QT interval initially).

stem: Insulin lowers K+ by:

choices.feedback: K+ is not chelated by any medication.
Insulin doesn't increase renal K+ excretion. That's diuretics.

choices.text: Driving K+ into cells via Na/K
choices.feedback: Correct! Insulin stimulates the Na+/K+-ATPase pump → K+ moves from ECF into cells. Give regular insulin IV with dextrose (to prevent hypoglycemia). Most RELIABLE shifting agent. Onset 15-30 min, lasts 4-6h. Monitor glucose q1h.

choices.text: Binding K+ in the GI tract
choices.feedback: That's the mechanism of Kayexalate/patiromer.

explanation: Insulin lowers serum potassium by driving K+ into cells via stimulation of the Na+/K+-ATPase pump on cell membranes. Insulin activates this pump, which moves 2 K+ ions into the cell for every 3 Na+ ions pumped out, shifting potassium from the extracellular to intracellular compartment. A common regimen is regular insulin IV along with dextrose to prevent hypoglycemia, with onset in 20-30 minutes and peak effect at 30-60 minutes. Insulin does NOT chelate potassium (chelation involves binding ions in the blood), does NOT increase renal excretion (that's the mechanism of loop diuretics), and does NOT bind K+ in the GI tract (that's the mechanism of kayexalate/patiromer/SZC). Glucose monitoring is essential post-administration, as hypoglycemia is a common and dangerous complication.

stem: #1 cause of hyperkalemia?

choices.feedback: Correct! The kidneys are responsible for 90% of K+ excretion. Impaired renal function is the #1 cause of hyperkalemia. Other common causes: ACEi/ARBs + spironolactone (especially in CKD patients), acidosis, cell lysis (rhabdomyolysis, TLS).
Dietary excess alone rarely causes hyperkalemia with normal kidney function.
Alkalosis causes HYPOkalemia (K+ shifts into cells).
Insulin LOWERS K+, not raises it.

explanation: Renal failure is the #1 cause of hyperkalemia. The kidneys are responsible for excreting approximately 90% of daily potassium load. When GFR declines below target rate, potassium excretion becomes impaired, leading to accumulation. StatPearls states: 'Acute and chronic kidney disease are common causes of hyperkalemia. Elevations in serum potassium typically do not occur until the glomerular filtration rate declines below target rate.' Excessive dietary K+ intake rarely causes hyperkalemia alone in patients with normal renal function. Alkalosis actually causes HYPOkalemia (not hyperkalemia) by shifting K+ INTO cells. Insulin therapy causes hypokalemia, not hyperkalemia — insulin deficiency (as in DKA) causes hyperkalemia.

stem: Sine wave pattern on EKG with K+ >8 indicates:

choices.feedback: Sine wave in context of known hyperkalemia is real and deadly.

choices.text: Typical finding — no action needed if asymptomatic
choices.feedback: Even 'asymptomatic' patients with sine wave are in immediate danger.

Correct! Sine wave = K+ >8.0, fusion of widened QRS with peaked T waves. VF or asystole imminent. IV calcium gluconate STAT + insulin/glucose + emergent hemodialysis. Defibrillation may be needed if VF develops.
Sine wave = pre-arrest rhythm. Immediate action needed.

explanation: A sine wave pattern on EKG with K+ >8 mEq/L indicates imminent cardiac arrest and requires emergent treatment. The sine wave represents the final EKG stage before asystole or ventricular fibrillation — the widened QRS merges with the T wave, creating a sinusoidal pattern that indicates loss of effective cardiac conduction. This is a true medical emergency requiring immediate IV calcium gluconate (cardiac membrane stabilization), followed by aggressive potassium-lowering therapies and likely emergent hemodialysis. This is NEVER artifact, NEVER a 'typical finding,' and NEVER appropriate for observation alone. Treatment should not be delayed for repeat testing or confirmatory labs.

stem: Which medications commonly CAUSE hyperkalemia?

choices.feedback: CCBs don't significantly affect potassium levels.
Diabetes medications don't typically cause hyperkalemia directly.
These cause HYPOkalemia from renal K+ wasting.

Correct! ACEi/ARBs reduce aldosterone → decreased K+ excretion. K+-sparing diuretics (spironolactone, eplerenone, amiloride, triamterene) directly block K+ secretion. The combination is particularly dangerous in CKD. Also: NSAIDs, beta-blockers, calcineurin inhibitors, heparin, TMP-SMX (blocks ENaC).

explanation: ACE inhibitors/ARBs combined with potassium-sparing diuretics (spironolactone, eplerenone, amiloride, triamterene) are a well-known combination that commonly causes hyperkalemia. ACEi/ARBs reduce aldosterone secretion, decreasing renal K+ excretion. K+-sparing diuretics independently block potassium secretion in the collecting duct. Together, the additive effect on K+ retention creates significant hyperkalemia risk. StatPearls lists 'medications that disrupt potassium homeostasis, such as potassium-sparing diuretics and renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibitors' as key causes. Calcium channel blockers do not significantly affect potassium. Metformin and glipizide do not cause hyperkalemia. Loop diuretics and thiazides cause HYPOkalemia (increased renal K+ excretion), not hyperkalemia.

stem: Pseudohyperkalemia should be suspected when:

choices.text: K+ is mildly elevated with symptoms
choices.feedback: Symptomatic hyperkalemia is usually real.

choices.text: K+ is elevated but EKG is normal and result is unexpected
choices.feedback: Correct! Pseudohyperkalemia: falsely elevated K+ from hemolyzed sample (most common), prolonged tourniquet, excessive fist clenching, extreme leukocytosis (>100K), thrombocytosis (>500K). Always repeat the test if unexpected elevation with normal EKG and no clinical cause.
K+ >8 with cardiac arrest is real hyperkalemia, not pseudo.

choices.text: Any hyperkalemia in a dialysis patient
choices.feedback: Dialysis patients frequently have real hyperkalemia.

explanation: Pseudohyperkalemia should be suspected when K+ is elevated but the EKG is normal and the result is clinically unexpected. Pseudohyperkalemia is an in vitro artifact caused by K+ release from cells during or after blood draw — NOT a true elevation of in vivo serum potassium. Common causes include hemolysis during phlebotomy (most common), prolonged tourniquet application, fist clenching during draw, extreme thrombocytosis (>500,000/μL), or leukocytosis (>100,000/μL). The key clinical clue: the patient has no EKG changes and no symptoms consistent with hyperkalemia. Management: redraw the specimen carefully (no tourniquet, no fist clenching, gentle handling) and recheck. Pseudohyperkalemia does NOT require treatment. However, K+ >8 with cardiac arrest is real hyperkalemia requiring emergent treatment, not pseudohyperkalemia.
references: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]

stem: For every 0.1 decrease in pH, K+ rises approximately:

choices.feedback: Correct! Acidosis causes transcellular K+ shift: H+ enters cells to be buffered → K+ exits to maintain electroneutrality. This means a patient in DKA with pH 7.0 may have artificially elevated K+ that will DROP dramatically once acidosis is corrected with insulin. Monitor K+ closely during correction.
This overstates the relationship.
While approximate, the 0.6 mEq/L per 0.1 pH rule is clinically useful.
The relationship is more pronounced than this.

explanation: For every 0.1 decrease in blood pH, serum potassium rises approximately 0.6 mEq/L. This occurs because acidosis (excess H+ ions) drives hydrogen ions INTO cells via the H+/K+ exchanger, and potassium moves OUT of cells to maintain electroneutrality. This is particularly relevant in metabolic acidosis (e.g., DKA, lactic acidosis, renal tubular acidosis). The clinical implication: a patient in DKA may have a 'normal' or elevated serum K+ despite massive total body potassium depletion — as acidosis is corrected with insulin and fluids, K+ will shift back into cells and the patient may develop life-threatening hypokalemia. This is why potassium replacement is started early in DKA management even when serum K+ appears normal. Note: this relationship is most predictable with mineral acidoses; organic acidoses (lactic, ketoacidosis) may show a less predictable relationship.
references: Simon LV, Rout P. Hyperkalemia. [Updated 2025 Dec 1]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470284/ [Open Access]
